narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score and choice to study full-time. However, the admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors from past students, estimated a 61.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam—above the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction was driven primarily by your lower rankings in school deciles for Year 1 and especially Year 3 grades, which stood out as the strongest signals pushing the model toward expecting a failure. While your LSAT score was a bright spot—well above average and the second-biggest factor pulling the prediction toward passing success—those early and later academic deciles outweighed it. Your undergraduate GPA, also below the typical level, added further concern in the same direction, though to a lesser degree. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your middle family income and full-time status were neutral or slightly positive but didn't shift the balance much. These patterns reflect how the model weighs consistent academic performance as key for bar success, where your deciles placed you below most successful applicants. It's tough to hear, especially knowing the extra hurdles many Black women face in these systems, but this isn't a judgment on your potential—it's one data-driven view. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, as that's the top lever here, and perhaps seek targeted LSAT or GPA enhancement resources tailored for underrepresented students. You could also explore schools with holistic reviews or bar prep support. Keep pushing; many have turned similar predictions around with focused effort. We're rooting for you.
